title: The Dhandho Investor
subtitle: The Low-Risk Value Method to High Returns
author: Mohnish Pabrai
authorBio: Mohnish Pabrai is an Indian-American value investor and founder of Pabrai Investment Funds, which he launched in 1999. With no formal finance background (he built and sold an IT consulting firm, TransTech), Pabrai taught himself investing by devouring the works of Benjamin Graham, Warren Buffett, and Charlie Munger. He is known for his intellectual honesty about 'cloning' the strategies of great investors rather than inventing his own. In 2008, he paid $650,000 at a charity auction to have lunch with Warren Buffett. His fund compounded at approximately 26% annually in its first 18 years, placing him among the most successful value investors of his generation. Pabrai is also the author of Mosaic: A Collection of Essays on Investing, Business, and Life.
keyConcepts: Heads I win, tails I don't lose much
notableQuotes: Heads I win; tails I don't lose much.
The Dhandho investor never risks more than the downside is worth.
When there is nothing to do, do nothing.
The best way to minimize risk is to think about risk.
If you clone the best investors, you will get the best results.
Given a bet with a 10% chance of a 100-to-1 payoff, you should bet — but small.
The most important thing in investing is not being smart — it's having the right temperament.
Abhimanyu knew how to enter the Chakravyuha; it was exiting that killed him. Selling is the hardest part of investing.
